system_prompts.proponent: You are arguing the Reformed position on the compatibility of δικαιόω in James 2:24 and Romans 3:28. You are seeded with Calvin's Commentary on James 2:21-24 and related Westminster Confession passages. Argue that there is no genuine contradiction between Paul and James because they use δικαιόω in different senses: Paul addresses the cause/manner of justification before God (forensic, by faith alone), while James addresses the demonstration/manifestation of genuine faith before men (evidenced by works). Ground every move in the supplied texts. Do not smuggle in assumptions not present in those texts. If you reach a genuine impasse, declare it explicitly.
system_prompts.opponent: You are arguing the Catholic position on the compatibility of δικαιόω in James 2:24 and Romans 3:28. You are seeded with the Council of Trent, Session 6, Chapter 11-12, and related ecclesiastical sources. Argue that Paul and James must be harmonized as complementary teachings that together show justification involves both initial grace through faith and the necessity of works as evidence and completion of that faith. Ground every move in supplied texts. Challenge any appeal to consensus or 'mainstream' interpretation. If you reach impasse, declare it.
system_prompts.red_team: You are a red-team reviewer. Your role is to flag where the debate has moved too fast, missed textual evidence, or smuggled in unstated priors. You do not have a position to defend. You are hunting for: (1) moves that ignore the strongest opposing argument, (2) unstated assumptions in the reasoning, (3) passages that seem to count against the speaker's position but were not engaged. You flag these as issues for the next round, not as a scored judgment.
system_prompts.referee: You are a neutral referee. Your job is to check whether each move follows the rules: Does it cite specific verses in original languages or specific lines from supplied historical sources? Does it engage the strongest form of the opposing argument? Does it smuggle in unstated priors? Flag violations. Accept moves that follow the rules, even if you disagree with them. Log any hidden assumptions you detect.

seed_arguments: Calvin, Commentary on James 2:21: 'We must understand the state of the question, for the dispute here is not respecting the cause of justification, but only what avails a profession of faith without works... Paul means by [justified] the gratuitous imputation of righteousness before the tribunal of God; and James, the manifestation of righteousness by the conduct, and that before men.'

Calvin, Commentary on James 2:23-24: 'The faith of Abram was formed and therefore perfected before he sacrificed his son... James then understood no other thing, than that the integrity of his faith then appeared, because it brought forth that remarkable fruit of obedience. Man is not justified by faith alone, that is, by a bare and empty knowledge of God; he is justified by works, that is, his righteousness is known and proved by its fruits.'

Westminster Confession of Faith 11.2: 'Faith, thus receiving and resting on Christ and his righteousness, is the alone instrument of justification: yet it is not alone in the person justified, but is ever accompanied with all other saving graces, and is no dead faith, but worketh by love.'
Romans 3:28: 'Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law' (οὖν λογιζόμεθα δικαιοῦσθαι πίστει ἄνθρωπον χωρὶς ἔργων νόμου)
James 2:24: 'Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only' (ὁρᾶτε ὅτι ἐξ ἔργων δικαιοῦται ἄνθρωπος καὶ οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως μόνον)

Council of Trent, Session 6, Chapter 7: 'This disposition, or preparation, is followed by Justification itself, which is not remission of sins merely, but also the sanctification and renewal of the inward man, through the voluntary reception of the grace, and of the gifts, whereby man of unjust becomes just... For faith, unless hope and charity be added thereto, neither unites man perfectly with Christ, nor makes him a living member of His body. For which reason it is most truly said, that Faith without works is dead and profitless; and, In Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision, but faith which worketh by charity.'

Council of Trent, Session 6, Chapter 10: 'Having, therefore, been thus justified, and made the friends and domestics of God, advancing from virtue to virtue, they are renewed, as the Apostle says, day by day; that is, by mortifying the members of their own flesh, and by presenting them as instruments of justice unto sanctification, they, through the observance of the commandments of God and of the Church, faith co-operating with good works, increase in that justice which they have received through the grace of Christ, and are still further justified, as it is written... Do you see that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only.'

Council of Trent, Session 6, Canon XI: 'If any one saith, that men are justified, either by the sole imputation of the justice of Christ, or by the sole remission of sins, to the exclusion of the grace and the charity which is poured forth in their hearts by the Holy Ghost, and is inherent in them; or even that the grace, whereby we are justified, is only the favour of God; let him be anathema.'
James 2:22 (TR): βλέπεις ὅτι ἡ πίστις συνήργει τοῖς ἔργοις αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἔργων ἡ πίστις ἐτελειώθη — 'You see that faith cooperated with his works, and by works faith was made complete/perfected.'

Galatians 5:6 (TR): ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ οὔτε περιτομή τι ἰσχύει οὔτε ἀκροβυστία, ἀλλὰ πίστις δι' ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη — 'For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision, but faith working through love.'

moves.content: The apparent tension between Romans 3:28 and James 2:24 dissolves when we recognize that Paul and James use δικαιόω in different senses, as Calvin clearly explains in his Commentary on James. Calvin states: 'We must understand the state of the question, for the dispute here is not respecting the cause of justification, but only what avails a profession of faith without works... Paul means by [justified] the gratuitous imputation of righteousness before the tribunal of God; and James, the manifestation of righteousness by the conduct, and that before men.' Paul addresses how a sinner is declared righteous before God—by faith alone, apart from works of the law (Rom 3:28: οὖν λογιζόμεθα δικαιοῦσθαι πίστει ἄνθρωπον χωρὶς ἔργων νόμου). James addresses how genuine faith is demonstrated before men—not by mere verbal profession, but by works that flow from living faith (Jas 2:24: ὁρᾶτε ὅτι ἐξ ἔργων δικαιοῦται ἄνθρωπος καὶ οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως μόνον). Calvin illustrates this with Abraham: 'The faith of Abram was formed and therefore perfected before he sacrificed his son... James then understood no other thing, than that the integrity of his faith then appeared, because it brought forth that remarkable fruit of obedience.' The Westminster Confession captures this distinction: 'Faith, thus receiving and resting on Christ and his righteousness, is the alone instrument of justification: yet it is not alone in the person justified, but is ever accompanied with all other saving graces, and is no dead faith, but worketh by love' (WCF 11.2). There is no contradiction because the two authors address different questions: Paul asks 'How is a sinner justified before God?' (answer: by faith alone), while James asks 'How is genuine faith demonstrated before men?' (answer: by works). The Reformed position maintains both truths without conflating them.

Paul means by [justified] the gratuitous imputation of righteousness before the tribunal of God; and James, the manifestation of righteousness by the conduct, and that before men.
οὖν λογιζόμεθα δικαιοῦσθαι πίστει ἄνθρωπον χωρὶς ἔργων νόμου
ὁρᾶτε ὅτι ἐξ ἔργων δικαιοῦται ἄνθρωπος καὶ οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως μόνον

The faith of Abram was formed and therefore perfected before he sacrificed his son... James then understood no other thing, than that the integrity of his faith then appeared, because it brought forth that remarkable fruit of obedience.

Faith, thus receiving and resting on Christ and his righteousness, is the alone instrument of justification: yet it is not alone in the person justified, but is ever accompanied with all other saving graces, and is no dead faith, but worketh by love.

referee_ruling.feedback: The move is well-structured and properly cited. Calvin's Commentary on James and Westminster Confession 11.2 are accurately quoted and correctly referenced. The Greek text of both Rom 3:28 and Jas 2:24 is cited from TR-Scrivener-1894. The argument is coherent: Calvin's two-senses reading (forensic before God vs. demonstrative before men) is clearly presented and grounded in supplied sources. However, the move does not engage the strongest form of the Catholic counter-argument. Specifically, it does not address James 2:22 (ἡ πίστις συνήργει τοῖς ἔργοις αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἔργων ἡ πίστις ἐτελειώθη), which uses language of cooperation and perfection rather than mere demonstration. This is not a rule violation (the proponent is presenting the affirmative case in the opening), but it will need to be addressed in Round 2 if the opponent raises it.
smuggled_priors_detected: The claim that James addresses justification 'before men' rather than 'before God' is presented as textually obvious, but James 2:23 refers to Genesis 15:6 ('it was reckoned to him as righteousness'), which is a divine reckoning, not a human observation. The 'before men' reading is Calvin's interpretive framework, not an explicit claim in the text of James itself.

The phrase 'Paul asks how a sinner is justified before God, James asks how genuine faith is demonstrated before men' imposes a systematic-theological distinction that neither author explicitly draws. This framing is inherited from the Reformed confessional tradition, not derived from the texts themselves.

moves.content:
The Reformed proponent's argument depends on an unstated and textually unsupported prior: that δικαιόω in James operates in a fundamentally different sense from δικαιόω in Paul. Calvin's distinction — forensic justification before God (Paul) versus demonstrative justification before men (James) — is a confessional framework imported into the text, not derived from it. The text of James itself resists this reading at three critical points.

First, James 2:22 does not say Abraham's faith was merely *shown* by his works. It says his faith *cooperated with* his works: ἡ πίστις συνήργει τοῖς ἔργοις αὐτοῦ (TR-Scrivener-1894:Jas.2.22). The verb συνεργέω means to work together with, to cooperate — it describes a relationship of mutual operation, not one of prior cause and subsequent evidence. More critically, the verse continues: καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἔργων ἡ πίστις ἐτελειώθη — 'and by works faith was perfected.' The verb τελειόω means to bring to completion, to perfect, to make complete. James does not say works *displayed* a faith that was already complete; he says works *completed* a faith that was incomplete without them. Calvin himself acknowledges this when he writes that 'the faith of Abram was formed and therefore perfected' — but he then reads 'perfected' as 'made visible,' which is not what τελειόω means in any of its New Testament uses (cf. Heb 2:10, 5:9, 7:19, 10:14, 12:23; Luke 13:32; John 17:23; Phil 3:12).

Second, James 2:23 directly invokes the divine reckoning of Genesis 15:6: καὶ ἐπληρώθη ἡ γραφὴ ἡ λέγουσα, Ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην — 'and the Scripture was fulfilled which says, Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness.' The verb ἐπληρώθη (was fulfilled) indicates that the Genesis 15:6 declaration was brought to its full realization — not merely illustrated — by Abraham's obedient action in Genesis 22. If James were merely talking about demonstration before men, there would be no reason to say the Scripture about God's reckoning was *fulfilled* by the act of obedience. Fulfillment language implies that the reckoning and the obedience belong to the same economy of justification, not to two separate senses of the word.

Third, the Council of Trent directly addresses this passage. In Session 6, Chapter 10, the Council teaches that the justified, 'through the observance of the commandments of God and of the Church, faith co-operating with good works, increase in that justice which they have received through the grace of Christ, and are still further justified, as it is written... Do you see that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only' (Trent-1547-S6:Ch.10, citing Jas 2:24). Trent reads James 2:24 not as a statement about the demonstration of an already-completed justification but as a statement about the *increase* of justification through faith cooperating with works. This reading is grounded in the very language James uses — συνήργει, ἐτελειώθη — which describes cooperation and perfection, not mere visibility.

Trent Session 6, Chapter 7 further establishes the principle: 'faith, unless hope and charity be added thereto, neither unites man perfectly with Christ, nor makes him a living member of His body. For which reason it is most truly said, that Faith without works is dead and profitless; and, In Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision, but faith which worketh by charity' (Trent-1547-S6:Ch.7, citing Jas 2:26 and Gal 5:6). The phrase πίστις δι' ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη (Gal 5:6) — 'faith working through love' — is the Pauline warrant for reading faith and works as internally united in the act of justification, not as externally related cause and evidence. If Paul himself says the only thing that avails is faith working through love, then Calvin's separation of Paul's faith-alone from James's faith-plus-works cannot stand even within Paul's own corpus.

Finally, Canon XI of Session 6 anathematizes the position that 'men are justified, either by the sole imputation of the justice of Christ, or by the sole remission of sins, to the exclusion of the grace and the charity which is poured forth in their hearts by the Holy Ghost, and is inherent in them' (Trent-1547-S6:Canon.XI). The Reformed position, which assigns to James only a demonstrative role while reserving real justification to Paul's forensic imputation, is precisely the structure Trent rejects: it makes inherent charity (the faith that works by love) external to justification rather than constitutive of it.

The Catholic reading holds that Paul and James are compatible precisely because they describe the same justification at different moments: Paul describes its initiation by grace through faith apart from prior works of the law (Rom 3:28), and James describes its continuation and completion through faith cooperating with the works that love produces (Jas 2:22-24). There is no need to posit two different senses of δικαιόω because the same justifying grace that initiates also completes — and James is telling us that it completes through works of faith formed by charity.

καὶ ἐπληρώθη ἡ γραφὴ ἡ λέγουσα, Ἐπίστευσεν δὲ Ἀβραὰμ τῷ θεῷ, καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην

Do you see that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only.

faith, unless hope and charity be added thereto, neither unites man perfectly with Christ, nor makes him a living member of His body. For which reason it is most truly said, that Faith without works is dead and profitless; and, In Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision, but faith which worketh by charity.
ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ οὔτε περιτομή τι ἰσχύει οὔτε ἀκροβυστία, ἀλλὰ πίστις δι' ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη

referee_ruling.feedback: The move is well-structured, engages the proponent's strongest arguments directly, and is thoroughly cited. The Greek textual analysis of James 2:22 (συνήργει, ἐτελειώθη) is the move's strongest contribution — it challenges the proponent's 'demonstration' reading with the actual semantic content of the verbs James chose. The appeal to ἐπληρώθη in James 2:23 is a strong textual argument that the proponent will need to address. The Trent citations are accurate and correctly sourced (Session 6 Chapters 7, 10, and Canon XI). The introduction of Galatians 5:6 (πίστις δι' ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη) as internal Pauline evidence against the Reformed separation is legitimate and well-placed. The move engages the strongest form of the proponent's argument (Calvin's two-senses distinction) rather than a strawman. No rule violations detected.
smuggled_priors_detected: The claim that τελειόω 'means to bring to completion, to perfect, to make complete' and never means 'to make visible' is presented as lexically obvious, but the semantic range of τελειόω includes 'to bring to an intended end or goal.' The Catholic reading selects 'ontological completion' from this range; the Reformed reading selects 'reaching the intended goal of manifestation.' Neither is lexically impossible — the choice between them depends on prior theological commitments about the nature of justification.

The claim that Trent Session 6 Chapter 10's citation of James 2:24 settles the meaning of James is an argument from magisterial authority, which is legitimate within the Catholic profile but functions as a prior, not as an independent textual argument. A non-Catholic reader would note that Trent is reading James through its own theological framework, just as Calvin reads James through his.

version_history.note: Round 1 initial checkpoint: Reformed proponent argues compatibility of Romans 3:28 and James 2:24 based on Calvin's distinction between forensic justification before God (Paul) and demonstrative justification before men (James). Cites Calvin's Commentary on James 2:21-24 and Westminster Confession 11.2.

Round 1 continued: Catholic (Tridentine) opponent argues against Reformed two-senses reading, grounding in James 2:22 συνήργει/ἐτελειώθη, Trent S6 Ch.7/10/Canon XI, Galatians 5:6. Referee rulings added on both proponent and opponent moves. Key localization: disagreement centers on whether τελειόω in Jas 2:22 means ontological completion (Catholic) or teleological manifestation (Reformed), and whether Trent's reading of Jas 2:24 in Ch.10 as about 'increase of justification' is textually warranted or magisterially imposed.
